title: Quantum Mpemba effect for operators in open systems
abstract: The quantum Mpemba effect concerns with anomalous relaxation of quantum states that evolves either under unitary or non-unitary dynamics. In the context of open quantum systems, while most studies focus on quantum states evolving under completely positive trace-presing dynamics described by the Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad (GKSL) master equation, we demonstrate that an analogous effect can arise at the level of operators. In particular, we show that operators that evolves under the adjoint Liouvillian -- despite not being a trace-preserving map -- can still exhibit a genuine Mpemba effect. We derive general conditions under which this phenomenon can occur and validate our predictions for three different open quantum setups. Our results broaden the scope of the Mpemba effect in quantum systems and provide a framework for controlling the relaxation of physically relevant observables.
summary: This paper shows that the Quantum Mpemba effect can be realized for physical observables by selectively removing the slowest decaying modes from an operator. This process can lead to an exponential speed-up in relaxation, even though the transformed operator starts further from the steady state. The effect is shown to be robust across various systems, including spin chains and bosonic lattices.
main_problem: Investigating whether the Quantum Mpemba effect (anomalous accelerated relaxation) can occur at the level of physical operators (observables) rather than just quantum states.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a 'mode-bypassing' procedure can induce a genuine Mpemba effect for operators, where a transformed operator relaxes faster to the steady state despite starting at a larger initial distance.
method: The study uses a mode-bypassing protocol to remove the slowest decay modes (SDM) from an operator and employs the 'dressed distance' (a trace distance under the Petz recovery map) to quantify relaxation in non-trace-preserving adjoint dynamics.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to open quantum systems described by the GKSL master equation, specifically including a single-qubit system, a non-equilibrium XX-spin chain, and a one-dimensional bosonic lattice subject to dephasing.
key_observables: Dressed distance (Ddd), expectation values of local and non-local operators (e.g., population imbalance, bond current, and string operators), and Pauli string decomposition coefficients.
important_parameters: Dephasing strength (gamma), tunneling amplitude (J), temperature (T), and chemical potential gradients.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be Markovian and described by the GKSL master equation under standard Born-Markov and secular approximations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the occurrence of the Mpemba effect via distance crossing in a single qubit, the relaxation of local and non-local operators in spin chains, and the numerical validation of theoretical bounds for the change in distance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the operator Mpemba effect, develops the mode-bypassing mathematical framework and distance measures, demonstrates the effect in single-qubit and spin-chain models, extends the analysis to bosonic lattices, discusses the implications for operator locality, and provides experimental measurement protocols.
why_interesting: It extends a well-known phenomenon of state relaxation to the level of observables, providing a new framework for controlling the relaxation of physical quantities in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Presence of the genuine operator Mpemba effect in a single qubit setup evolving under a unital map. (a) Time evolution of the dressed distance for the original operator O = b0I + ⃗b · ⃗σ and the transformed operator e O, obtained by bypassing the SDM. Although e O is initially farther from the asymptotic value, it relaxes significantly faster due to the removal of the SDM. (b) Time evolution of the corresponding expectation values evaluated in the initial state |ψ0⟩= |+⟩, demonstrating the accelerated relaxation of the transformed operator e O. The parameters used are ω0 = 0.5, γ = 1, b0 = 1, and the vector ⃗b is chosen randomly with values for the com- ponents given as (−0.01954,...

FIG. 2. Results for the Non-equilibrium XX-spin chain: Demonstration of the operator Mpemba effect for operators with different degree of locality for the boundary-driven spin chain with N = 4. (a) Local operator: population imbalance O = n2 −n3. The transformed operator e O exhibits faster convergence to the steady state despite initially larger dressed distance. (b) Dynamics for the corresponding expectation val- ues starting with the initial state |ψ0⟩= |1⟩⊗4, (|1⟩is the spin down state) showing accelerated relaxation and convergence to the same steady state. (c) Two-site operator: current I1,2. The transformed operator eI1,2 relaxes exponentially faster than I1,2, despite a larger...

FIG. 3. Results for one-dimensional bosonic lattice subjected to dephasing: (a) The transformed operator en2(t) initially lies farther from the steady state value n(ss) 2 in terms of dressed distance (for this case it reduces to trace distance, as the map is unital), yet it relaxes more rapidly than the local number density n2 at the second site. In contrast, n2, al- though closer to equilibrium at initial times, exhibits slower relaxation. This counterintuitive behavior highlights the pres- ence of the genuine Mpemba effect for operators.
